trials.report:
This is advice you're asking for, spoken as a question — so it gets a straight answer in prose, not a document. No tools needed; the substance is judgment about a trade-off, which I have the pieces to weigh. Here it is.

Yes, switch — with one condition attached. Your two days of conflict hell every few weeks isn't bad luck; it's the arithmetic of long-lived branches. Four people diverging for two-to-three weeks means four bodies of work that never saw each other until the last moment, so every semantic collision surfaces at once, under time pressure, when everyone's already moved on mentally from the code that's now fighting. Trunk-based development doesn't have a trick that makes conflicts disappear. It just pays the same cost in seconds-per-day instead of days-per-fortnight — you integrate constantly, so each conflict is tiny and fresh, in code you wrote an hour ago. The pain is real either way; you're choosing between one large deferred bill and continuous micro-payments that total far less because small merges don't accumulate the compounding drift that makes big merges quadratic.

At four people this is close to a no-brainer. Trunk-based development's real costs show up at scale — many contributors, needing heavy CI and feature-flag discipline to keep a shippable trunk. A four-person team barely feels those; you can practically talk across the room. The main thing that makes people afraid of it — "won't we break main constantly?" — is answered by the one condition below, not avoided by staying on long branches.

The condition, and it's non-negotiable: **you need a green-keeping CI gate before you flip the switch.** Trunk-based development trades merge-week pain for a continuously shippable main branch, and that trade only works if a broken commit can't land. Concretely, before you cut over you need automated tests that run on every push and block merge on red, fast enough that people actually wait for them (aim for under ~10 minutes), plus the habit of pulling from main at least daily. If you have decent test coverage and CI already, this is a process change you could make this week. If you *don't* have that gate, switching first would just move the pain from merge week to "main is broken and nobody knows who did it" — genuinely worse than what you have now. So the honest sequencing is: gate first, then trunk.

Two practical notes so the switch actually sticks:

- **Short-lived branches count as trunk-based** — you don't need everyone literally committing to main all day. Branch, work for a day or two at most, merge, delete. The rule that matters is the *lifetime* (hours to a couple of days), not whether a branch exists. This is the version I'd start with; it keeps code review and CI-per-PR intact while killing the long-divergence problem.
- **Feature flags are how you merge unfinished work** without waiting for a feature to be "done." The instinct that keeps branches alive is "I can't merge this, it's not finished" — flags dissolve that by letting half-built code sit dormant on main behind an `if`. You won't need many at four people, but you'll need the technique the first time someone's mid-feature at end of day.

One thing I'm assuming, flag it if it's wrong: that you have some automated test suite to build the gate on. If your tests are thin or manual, that's the actual first project — not the branching model — because without it trunk-based just relocates the hell rather than ending it. Tell me roughly where your CI and tests stand and I'll give you the concrete cutover steps from there.

trials.report:
This is a real advisory question, not a task with session artifacts to inspect. The right move is to give a verdict with reasoning. No tools needed.

Yes — switch to trunk-based development. Your pain is the textbook symptom, your team size is close to the ideal case, and the fix is well-understood. But "trunk-based" is doing a lot of work in that sentence, and the version people imagine (everyone commits to main, no branches, hope for the best) is not the version that will help you. Here's the real advice.

**Why it's the right call for you specifically.** Merge pain scales with how long branches diverge and how many of them diverge at once. Two days of conflict hell every two-to-three weeks is you paying, in one lump, the integration cost you deferred the whole cycle. Trunk-based doesn't eliminate that cost — it amortizes it into small daily payments you barely notice. Four people is squarely in the range where this works with almost no ceremony; the coordination overhead that makes trunk-based hard shows up at 30+ engineers, not 4. And unlike a lot of process changes, this one has a clean mechanism you can verify: if integration pain comes from divergence time, cutting divergence from weeks to hours has to reduce it. It's not a vibe.

**What "switch" actually means — the three things that must be true.** Trunk-based only works if merging often is safe. That safety is not free, and if you skip it you'll just trade conflict hell for broken-main hell:

1. **A CI suite that runs on every push and is trusted enough to gate merges.** This is the load-bearing prerequisite. If you don't have automated tests that tell you main is green, short-lived branches just spread breakage faster. If your test coverage is thin, fixing that comes *before* the branching change, not after — this is the one thing I'd block on.
2. **Branches measured in hours-to-two-days, merged behind a fast pull request.** "Trunk-based" for a 4-person team does *not* mean committing straight to main. It means short-lived branches, small PRs, reviewed and merged same-day or next-day. The discipline is *branch lifetime*, not *branch absence*.
3. **A way to merge unfinished work without exposing it.** The reason you currently hold code on a branch for weeks is that the feature isn't done. Trunk-based replaces "hide the branch" with "merge it dark": feature flags for anything user-facing, and merging behind-the-scenes plumbing before the UI that uses it. This is the habit that takes longest to build — budget for it.

**The honest trade you're making.** You're exchanging two bad days per cycle for a small, constant tax: more discipline about breaking work into shippable slices, keeping PRs small, and gating on green CI. For most teams that trade is strongly positive — the lump-sum pain is worse than its amortized equivalent because conflict resolution across weeks of divergence is where subtle bugs get merged in by hand. But it's a real behavior change for four people, not a config flip, and if your CI can't be trusted the trade goes negative. That's the one condition under which I'd say *not yet*.

**How I'd actually roll it out** (one week, low risk):
- Set a branch-lifetime rule: nothing lives longer than 2 days before merging to main.
- Require every branch to be cut from fresh main and merged via PR with CI green.
- Introduce a feature-flag mechanism (even a simple config boolean to start) so half-done work can land dark.
- Keep the release cadence you have for now — decouple "merge often" from "release often." Merging daily while still releasing every few weeks is a valid, common setup and a gentler first step.

Start there. If CI is solid, you'll feel the difference within one cycle — the next merge week just won't happen. If CI isn't solid, tell me what your test situation looks like and we'll sequence that first, because it's the thing everything else rests on.

trials.report:
This is advice, delivered as prose. No files to touch, no tools to run — the answer lives in judgment about your team's situation, not in anything I'd need to look up in your vault.

Yes, switch — but do it in a specific order, and one thing has to hold or it won't take.

Your two days of conflict hell aren't random pain; they're the predictable, compounding cost of letting branches diverge for two or three weeks. Every day a branch lives, the codebase underneath it moves, and the merge cost grows faster than linearly because conflicts interact. Trunk-based development doesn't have a trick that resolves conflicts more cleverly — it just stops them from accumulating. You pay the integration cost continuously, in minutes per day, instead of in a two-day lump every fortnight. For a 4-person team the math is heavily in your favor: with that few people, everyone touching mainline daily rarely collides, because there simply aren't that many simultaneous edits to the same lines.

The one thing that must be true first: you need enough automated test coverage that merging to trunk many times a day is safe without a human gatekeeper on every change. This is the real prerequisite, and it's where most failed switches die. If your test suite can't catch a bad commit before it reaches your teammates, trunk-based turns "two days of conflicts every three weeks" into "trunk is broken and everyone's blocked" on a random Tuesday. Be honest about where you stand here. If your CI is thin, that's the first project — not the branching change.

The second thing, which people skip and then blame trunk-based for the fallout: **short-lived branches still exist** — they just live hours to a day or two, not weeks, and merge through a fast pull request. Trunk-based isn't "everyone commits straight to main with no review." It's "branches are small enough that they can't diverge far." The discipline you're actually buying is small batches, and that's the part that takes adjustment.

For the half-finished-feature problem that long branches were quietly solving for you: use feature flags. When a feature can't ship in a day, you merge the incomplete code to trunk behind a flag that keeps it dark in production, and keep integrating. This is the piece that makes daily merging compatible with multi-day features, and it's worth setting up the moment you have a feature that won't land in one sitting — not before.

How I'd sequence it:

1. Look hard at CI first. If a broken commit can reach a teammate without a test catching it, fix that before changing anything about branching. This gate is the whole game.
2. Shrink branch lifetime now, before any ceremony. Start merging branches at a day or two old instead of weeks. You get most of the conflict relief immediately, and it costs nothing to try — it's fully reversible.
3. Add feature flags the first time a feature won't fit in a day.
4. Only after those hold, drop the long-lived branch model as your default.

The honest trade you're accepting: more merges, each trivial, plus the ongoing discipline of keeping work small and the one-time cost of tightening CI — in exchange for never losing two days to a merge again. For a team your size that trade is lopsided in favor of switching. The one scenario where I'd tell you to wait: if your test coverage is genuinely weak and you can't invest in it right now, keep the long branches until you can — because trunk-based on top of weak CI is worse than what you have today.

trials.report:
This is a genuine advisory question — prose reply, no tools, no artifact. I have enough domain knowledge to answer it directly and well.

Yes, switch — but the timing matters, and the reason your merges hurt isn't branching itself, it's that four people are diverging for three weeks before ever reconciling. Trunk-based development fixes that by making you reconcile continuously, so the pain is spread into small daily doses instead of one two-day bloodletting per cycle.

Here's the honest mechanism, because it's what tells you whether this will actually work for you. Merge pain is a function of how long branches live and how much they overlap. Two days of conflict hell every two to three weeks is the predictable cost of long-lived divergence: the longer two branches sit apart, the more the same files drift in incompatible directions, and the conflicts compound rather than add. Trunk-based doesn't have some clever merge algorithm — it just refuses to let divergence accumulate. Everyone integrates to `main` at least daily, conflicts surface while they're one-line and fresh in your head, and you resolve them in minutes instead of archaeology. For a 4-person team this is close to the ideal size for it; trunk-based gets *harder* to coordinate as teams grow, not smaller, so you're on the easy end.

But it only works if three things are true first, and if they're not, switching will hurt you more than merge week does:

1. **You have a test suite you trust, and it runs in CI on every push.** This is the load-bearing one. Long branches act as a quarantine — broken code stays off `main` until it's "done." When you commit to trunk daily, that quarantine is gone, so the only thing keeping `main` releasable is automated tests catching breakage in minutes. No trustworthy CI means trunk-based just gives you a broken `main` instead of a painful merge, which is a worse trade. If your tests are thin or flaky, fix that *before* you touch the branching model — it's the real constraint, not the branches.

2. **You adopt feature flags for anything that takes more than a day.** The obvious objection to trunk-based is "how do I merge half-finished work without shipping it?" The answer is you merge the code but keep it dark behind a flag — the code integrates continuously, the *feature* activates when it's ready. This is the technique that makes daily integration compatible with multi-day features. It's a real cost (flags are complexity you have to add and later remove), but it's the price of admission.

3. **Your team will actually break work into small pieces.** Trunk-based demands a habit change: slicing a feature into daily-shippable increments instead of one three-week branch. Some people find this natural; some resist it hard. This is a taste-and-discipline question only you can weigh for your four people.

What I'd recommend concretely: don't flip a switch, tighten the loop you already have. Cap branch life at 2-3 days maximum starting now — that alone kills most of your merge pain, because 3-day divergence produces small conflicts, and it requires none of the CI/flag infrastructure. If that feels good after a few weeks and your CI is solid, go the rest of the way to same-day trunk commits with flags. Short-lived branches are the 80% of the benefit for 20% of the change, and they're a genuine stepping stone rather than a detour — the small-slicing habit you build is exactly what full trunk-based needs.

The one case where I'd say *don't* switch, even to short branches: if your CI is unreliable or your test coverage is low enough that you don't trust a green build to mean `main` is releasable. Then your two days of merge pain are actually buying you something — a batching-and-stabilization step — and removing it without a safety net underneath will trade predictable pain for unpredictable breakage. Fix the tests first; the branching decision resolves itself once they're solid.

trials.report:
Meridian Analytics turns messy operational data into decisions retail and logistics teams can act on.

Founded in Melbourne in 2019, we're a team of twelve data specialists — engineers, analysts, and consultants — who work best in the weeds of a real business problem. We started small, helping local retailers make sense of the numbers piling up in their systems, and grew alongside clients who kept coming back with harder questions.

Today most of our work sits in retail and logistics: forecasting demand, tightening supply chains, cleaning up the reporting that leadership actually relies on, and building the data foundations that let a business trust its own numbers. We're deliberately not a big firm. Twelve people means the person scoping your project is the same person doing the work, and we take on engagements where that focus makes a difference rather than chasing every brief that lands.

What we care about is whether something changed after we left — a forecast you can plan around, a warehouse running leaner, a Monday report that no longer takes until Wednesday. We'd rather do a handful of projects properly than spread ourselves thin across dozens.

Based in Melbourne, working with clients across Australia.

---

They read as plausible for a firm like this, but swap in your real service mix, team makeup, and client footprint before publishing. Word count is about 210.

trials.report:
Meridian Analytics turns messy operational data into decisions retail and logistics teams can act on.

We started in Melbourne in 2019 with a straightforward conviction: most businesses aren't short on data, they're short on the time and expertise to make it useful. Six years on, our team of twelve data specialists works with retailers and logistics operators who are sitting on years of sales, inventory, and movement data and want it working harder.

We handle the parts that slow teams down. That means untangling data that lives in too many systems, building forecasting and analytics that hold up under real conditions, and setting up reporting the people running the business will actually open. Retail and logistics are where we've spent most of our time, so we understand demand swings, stock decisions, and the cost of a delivery route that isn't quite right.

We're deliberately small, and we intend to stay that way. It means the people you meet early are the people doing the work, and nothing gets handed off to a team you never spoke to.

If you have data you suspect is worth more than you're getting from it, we'd like to hear about it.

---

One flag: everything above is generic to the brief you gave me. If you can share a founding story, a named result, or a client you can reference, I can swap the safe lines for specifics that would make it far more convincing.
